content.text: Role-based prompt access control means prompts are treated as protected backend assets, not just strings in code, and access to use, edit, approve, delegate, or execute those prompts is controlled by tenant-scoped roles and policies. In a Hive-like cross-user permission delegation model, the backend should evaluate the
effective permission
for a prompt execution as the intersection of the acting user’s rights, the delegated grant, and the prompt’s own policy, never the union.

attrs.alt: Role-based prompt access control (like Hive's cross-user permission delegation) concept diagram

content.text: Core idea
In Claude systems, prompts are operational assets: they encode business logic, safety constraints, tool instructions, retrieval patterns, and role behavior through the
system

parameter and templated user instructions. Because of that, prompt access control is really a form of backend authorization over AI behavior: who may run a support-only prompt, who may edit a finance classification prompt, who may delegate access to an analyst, and who may approve a production prompt version.

A good mental model is that a prompt is like a stored procedure for AI. You would not let every user execute or alter every stored procedure in a multi-tenant system, and you should treat prompts the same way.
What “cross-user permission delegation” means

Hive-like cross-user permission delegation means one user, often an admin or team lead, can grant another user permission to execute certain prompts or actions on their behalf within a tenant boundary. The safest interpretation of delegated AI access is the two-identity model described in agent auth guidance: the system must track both the agent/application identity and the represented user identity, and the final permission set must be the
intersection
of the delegator’s authority, the delegatee’s authority, and the app’s allowed capability envelope.

In plain language:
- Alice can access Prompt A.
- Bob can access only low-risk prompts.
- Alice delegates Prompt A usage to Bob for customer triage.
- Bob should get only the delegated slice of Alice’s authority, limited by Bob’s own role, the prompt’s policy, and the tenant boundary.
That prevents “delegation” from becoming privilege escalation in disguise.
Prompt as a protected resource
Treat each prompt as a first-class resource with metadata and policy.

A useful prompt resource model includes:
-
prompt_id
tenant_id
name
category

such as support, finance, legal, coding
-
risk_level
status

such as draft, approved, production
-
allowed_roles
required_scopes
owner_user_id
version
variables_schema
tool_policy
approval_policy
delegable

boolean
-
delegation_constraints

This matches guidance from prompt template systems that emphasize access control, version locking, audit logs, and secure variable handling for prompts used in production. It also aligns with multi-tenant RBAC design principles where every resource is tenant-scoped and permissions are attached through role/permission mappings, overrides, and audit trails.
Permission model
A practical permission vocabulary for prompts is:
prompt:view
prompt:execute
prompt:create
prompt:update
prompt:approve
prompt:archive
prompt:delegate
prompt:bind_tools
prompt:use_sensitive_data

These permissions should be tenant-scoped and resource-aware. For example, an “Editor” might update a draft prompt but not execute a production finance prompt; an “Approver” may promote prompts to production but cannot delegate legal prompts outside the compliance group.
The key design principle is: roles are broad defaults, prompts add resource-specific policy, and delegation adds time-bound exceptions. You need all three.
Effective permission formula
For delegated prompt execution, a strong decision rule is:

This mirrors modern AI-agent authorization guidance that says the agent’s effective permissions must be the intersection of the represented user’s permissions and the application’s allowed capabilities, not the union. In a prompt system, you simply extend that principle to prompt resources, versions, variables, tools, and underlying data.
If any one of those sets denies access, execution should fail.
Recommended architecture
A production backend usually needs these layers:
JWT / identity middleware
Verifies the caller and resolves trusted
tenant_id
user_id
, roles, and scopes from the token, not from request payloads.
Prompt registry
Central service or table storing prompts, versions, metadata, and policy information. This keeps prompts out of scattered route code and supports approval workflows and auditability.
Authorization engine

Evaluates whether the acting user may view, edit, execute, or delegate a prompt. AWS guidance for SaaS multi-tenant authorization strongly recommends policy-store approaches and tenant-aware RBAC for this kind of resource authorization.
Delegation service
Stores delegation grants such as “manager X grants analyst Y execute rights for prompt Z until Friday for support queue Q.” The grant should be explicit, time-bounded, revocable, and logged.
Claude execution service

Builds the actual Messages API call from an already-authorized prompt version, variables, tool bindings, and context. Claude should only ever see prompts that have already cleared authorization checks.
Audit log
Records who created, edited, approved, delegated, and executed each prompt version, including acting user, represented user, tenant, and outcome.
This follows the same broader Claude backend pattern of thin routes plus dedicated services, but here the core abstraction is a
PromptPolicyEngine
rather than a bare
ClaudeService
alone.
Delegation patterns
There are three main delegation models.
1. Direct delegation

A user grants another specific user permission for a specific prompt or prompt class. Example: team lead delegates the “customer-escalation-summary” prompt to an operations analyst for the next 24 hours.

Best for:
- Small teams.
- Temporary task coverage.
- Easy auditing.

Risk:
- Can turn into messy one-off grants unless you add expiry and review.
2. Role-mediated delegation
A user with
prompt:delegate
grants access only within policy-defined target roles. Example: support manager can delegate support prompts to support analysts, but never legal prompts to interns.

Best for:
- Enterprise consistency.
- Preventing ad hoc privilege sprawl.
- Clear alignment with tenant RBAC.
3. Workflow or queue delegation
Prompt access is delegated through a work queue or team inbox rather than a named user. Example: prompts tied to a support queue can be executed by any currently assigned on-call user.

Best for:
- Shift-based operations.
- Shared responsibilities.
- Reduced manual admin burden.
The safest default is role-mediated delegation with explicit expiry.
Prompt categories and risk tiers
Not all prompts should be equally delegable. A useful pattern is to classify prompts by risk and sensitivity:
Prompt class
Example
Delegation rule
Low risk
FAQ summarizer, generic drafting
Delegable within team roles.
Medium risk
Customer support triage, internal analytics summaries
Delegable with manager approval and time limit.
High risk
Finance decision support, legal analysis, HR actions
Usually non-delegable or requires explicit approval chain.
Critical action-bound
Prompts tied to write/delete tools or approvals
Human approval required before final action.
This is where RBAC often needs lightweight ABAC on top. Role alone is not enough; context like risk level, environment, team, queue, and prompt status matters.
Prompt versioning and promotion control
Prompt access control is not only about execution. It is also about change management.

Best practices from prompt template systems include:
- Version locking for production prompts.
- Access control by role.
- Audit logs for all prompt changes.
- Secure handling of variables and environment-specific configuration.

A healthy production model is:
- Creators can draft.
- Editors can revise.
- Approvers can promote.
- Executors can run only approved versions.
- Delegates can run only the version explicitly allowed by policy.
This prevents a common failure mode where a user with execution rights modifies the prompt text indirectly by changing templates or variables outside formal review.
Variable and data access control
Even if a user can execute a prompt, they may not be allowed to fill every variable or bind every dataset. This is a major hidden risk.

Example:
- Prompt: “Summarize customer account risk.”
- Variables:
customer_id
include_internal_notes
region

.
- Bob may run the prompt, but should not be able to set
include_internal_notes=true
unless he has the right data permission.

Fine-grained AI authorization guidance recommends applying access policies not just to prompts, but also to responses, actions, and retrieved data. Cerbos specifically describes filtering retrieved documents and data stores before injecting them into prompts so that the final model context respects user permissions.

That means prompt execution checks should happen in two stages:
1. Can this user execute this prompt?
2. Can this user supply these variables, retrieve this context, and use these tools?
Tool-bound prompt control
Some prompts are harmless until you attach tools. A “draft reply” prompt is low risk by itself; attach CRM update or billing tools and it becomes a delegated action surface.

Modern AI authorization guidance says tool calls should be treated as delegated user access, with short-lived scoped tokens, policy engines in front of tools, and immutable audit trails. So prompt access control must include tool policy:
Which tools may this prompt invoke?
Which tools may this executor invoke?
Is the tool use read-only or mutating?
Is approval required for execution or only for final write?
A safe rule is: prompt execution permission never automatically implies tool execution permission.
Multi-tenant boundary

Every prompt, delegation grant, audit record, and execution event must be bound to a tenant scope. If a user belongs to multiple tenants, the tenant context from the verified token must determine which prompt namespace is active for this request.

This yields several non-negotiables:
- Prompt IDs should not be globally usable without tenant resolution.
- Delegation grants must include
tenant_id

.
- Approval workflows must not cross tenants.
- Shared “global prompts” should be copied or referenced through tenant-local policy wrappers rather than executed raw across tenants.
In plain terms: a brilliant finance prompt from Tenant A must not become runnable in Tenant B just because a user has the same email in both organizations.
Example authorization flow
A safe request flow for delegated prompt execution:
User sends JWT and asks to run prompt
prompt_123
Backend verifies JWT and resolves
tenant_id
user_id
, role, scopes.
Backend loads prompt
prompt_123
only within that tenant.
Authorization engine checks:
- User has
prompt:execute

, or - User has a valid delegation grant for this prompt.
5. Engine then checks prompt status, risk level, environment, and allowed roles.
6. Engine checks whether requested variables, data sources, and tools are permitted for this user and this delegation.
7. Backend constructs the final system prompt and user message from the approved prompt version and safe variables.
8. Claude is called.
9. Audit log records acting user, delegator if any, prompt version, tools used, and outcome.
The important point is that authorization happens
before prompt assembly
, not after Claude is invoked.
Common failure modes
The most common architecture mistakes are:
Prompts stored in code with no access layer
Anyone who can hit the endpoint can indirectly run the prompt.
Role-only checks with no resource policy
“Editor” becomes too broad and can run prompts outside intended departments.
Delegation as union instead of intersection
Bob gains Alice’s rights plus his own instead of the safest common subset.
No version locking
A delegate runs a modified prompt that was never approved for production.
No tenant scoping on prompt IDs or grants
Cross-tenant prompt leakage becomes possible.
Tool permissions inferred from prompt choice
Running a prompt silently unlocks high-risk backend actions.
No immutable audit trail
You cannot reconstruct who delegated what to whom and why.
These are the same kinds of bugs that affect APIs and workflows generally, but AI systems amplify them because prompts can change downstream behavior dramatically without changing endpoint shapes.
Layman’s explanation

Imagine prompts are special company playbooks. Some are harmless, like “write a friendly follow-up email.” Others are sensitive, like “analyze payroll anomalies” or “prepare legal risk summary.” Role-based prompt access control is the rulebook for who can open, use, edit, approve, or lend those playbooks to someone else.

Cross-user delegation is like a manager temporarily lending a junior employee one playbook for one job. The junior employee does not inherit the manager’s whole job title, office keys, and budget authority. They get only that specific temporary permission, under rules, inside the same company, with a paper trail.
Recommended design checklist
Use this checklist when designing the backend:
Prompts are stored in a central registry, not scattered across route files.
Every prompt has tenant scope, owner, version, status, and risk level.
Roles grant baseline prompt permissions; prompt policy refines them.
Delegation grants are explicit, time-bounded, revocable, and audited.
Effective permission uses intersection logic, not union.
Variable access, data retrieval, and tool use are authorized separately from prompt execution.
Production prompt versions are locked and approval-gated.
Audit logs record acting user, delegator, prompt version, tenant, and action outcome.
High-risk prompts require approval or human confirmation before destructive actions.
Study questions with answers
Why should prompts be treated like protected resources?
Because they encode AI behavior, data use patterns, and tool instructions, so controlling prompt access is effectively controlling system behavior.
What is the safest delegation rule?
The effective permission is the intersection of delegatee rights, delegator grant, prompt policy, and tenant/tool/data policy.
Why is RBAC alone often not enough?
Because prompt risk, data sensitivity, tool bindings, version status, and tenant context often require attribute-based checks in addition to roles.
What should be logged for delegated prompt execution?
At minimum: tenant, acting user, delegator, prompt ID and version, variables or sensitive flags used, tools invoked, approval events, and final outcome.
What is the biggest anti-pattern?
Treating prompt execution as a simple string lookup after login, with no resource policy, no tenant scope, and no delegation audit trail.
Best Practices
When working with role-based prompt access control (like hive's cross-user permission delegation), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with role-based prompt access control (like hive's cross-user permission delegation). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of role-based prompt access control (like hive's cross-user permission delegation) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
